Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

military_service.notes: EH: 'He served in the Quarter-master's Department in the Revolutionary War.'

narrative_blocks.header: Quartermaster's Department veteran; lived ninety years
narrative_blocks.body: Gilbert Hooker served in the Quartermaster's Department during the Revolutionary War, then settled at Stillwater, New York with his wife Rebecca Andrews where their children were born, later moving the family to Clyde, New York — and lived to see all of it: the nation he helped supply through the Revolution, the expansion across New York State, the rise of his son James Harvey to wealthy merchant at Troy. He died January 9, 1849 at approximately ninety years of age, having outlived his wife Rebecca by eight years — born in Woodbury, Connecticut in 1758, dying when Abraham Lincoln was still an Illinois congressman and the gold rush was just beginning in California, his life spanning the entire arc from Puritan New England colony to young republic.

burial.plot_notes: Maple Grove Cemetery Clyde Wayne County NY per FA

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Colonel James H. Hooker by Nelson Cook, ca. 1845-1848. Private Collection, California. Restored 2026.

burial.plot_notes: Oakwood Cemetery Troy NY per private collection research

notable.notable_blurb: Erie Canal and Hudson River transportation tycoon

career.organization: Troy Tow-Boat Line; Troy and Whitehall Towing Company; Northern Transportation Line; Pattison & Hart
career.notes: Started as business clerk at Pattison & Hart after moving to Troy ~1820. Built extensive freight operation on Erie, Oswego, and Champlain Canals, Lake Champlain, and Hudson River. By 1851: 11 barges (5,100 tons), 120 canal boats to Buffalo/Rochester/Oswego/Whitehall, 11 sail vessels and steam propeller 'James H. Hooker' on Lake Champlain, 6 steamboats + 5 propellers on Buffalo Canal Line, 800 horses on Erie Canal, 50 agents throughout NY state. Never married.

narrative_blocks.header: A steamship on Lake Champlain bore his name
narrative_blocks.body: By 1850, Colonel James H. Hooker operated one of the most extensive freight transportation networks in the American northeast — 50 canal boats to Buffalo, 29 to Rochester, 16 to Oswego, 25 to Whitehall, eleven barges totaling 5,100 tons, six steamboats and five propellers on the Buffalo Canal Line, eleven sail vessels on Lake Champlain, and a steam propeller named the 'James H. Hooker' carrying 300 tons between Albany and Montreal.

narrative_blocks.header: Violent in his speech; fair with his workers
narrative_blocks.body: James Harvey Hooker never married — his extended family knew him as 'Uncle James' while 20th century descendants called him 'the Fat Man' for his portly girth — and contemporaries described him as 'a passionate eccentric, violent in his speech and action, but ultimately fair and just with his workers and associates.' His portrait, painted by Nelson Cook in the period 1845-1848 when Cook was at his mature peak, survived in a California private collection and was cleaned and restored in February 2026 by Susan Brown-Madorsky of SB Conservation in Laguna Beach — 175 years after its subject died in Troy on December 28, 1851.

burial.plot_notes: Crown Hill Cemetery Indianapolis Marion County Indiana per FA

narrative_blocks.header: Died at thirty-one; widow renamed herself Whitton
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Hooker died March 25, 1866 in Indianapolis at thirty-one — leaving Clementine Russell Hooker with four children under eight, the youngest, Henry Samuel, barely a year old — and within a few years she had remarried, becoming Clementine Russell Hooker Whitton, a name that preserved and then buried her first husband's in the same breath. She outlived him by eleven years, dying in 1877; their son James Harvey survived to 1931, their daughter Margaret to 1952, meaning Henry Hooker's children outlasted him by decades in the same Indianapolis streets where he had died young.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

narrative_blocks.header: He died in the army — details unknown
narrative_blocks.body: George Hooker was born Christmas Eve 1838 in Buffalo, New York, and EH records his death with the terse notation 'd. in the army' — meaning he died during Civil War service, though whether by battle, disease, accident, or wounds is not specified. He was one of at least two soldiers in this immediate family; his uncle Gilbert Hooker had served in the Quartermaster's Department in the Revolutionary War, and George died young in the army of the republic that war had made.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

burial.plot_notes: Crown Hill Cemetery Indianapolis per FA

military_service.unit: Company H, 12th Regiment, Indiana Volunteer Infantry
military_service.notes: Sgt. Major, Company H, 12th Indiana Infantry. Same company as PVT John Brooks (I01044), whose widow Louisa Vanzant Jack later married. Post-war: Stockbridge Marble Co., Massachusetts per FA.

narrative_blocks.header: He married the widow of his company's Private
narrative_blocks.body: Sergeant Major John 'Jack' Hooker served in Company H of the 12th Indiana Volunteer Infantry during the Civil War; Private John Brooks served in the same company and died April 22, 1864 at Scottsboro, Alabama, buried at Chattanooga National Cemetery. After the war ended, Jack Hooker married Brooks's widow, Louisa Vanzant — a connection that collapsed the distance between survival and loss into a single family, the sergeant major and the dead private joined permanently through the woman who had belonged to both.

bio.bio_blurb: Hooker descendant who tracked a 250-year-old family clock

burial.plot_notes: West Clarendon Cemetery Clarendon Calhoun County Michigan per FA

narrative_blocks.header: Thomas Hooker’s clock was still keeping time
narrative_blocks.body: A clock the Hartford founder carried from England in the 1630s was still ticking in a Grand Rapids parlor when her obituary ran in 1885 — two and a half centuries, an ocean, and a continent later, passed sideways through cousins rather than down a single line.

sources.label: FindAGrave

burial.plot_notes: Obituary says buried in Burlingame beside his wife — likely not Michigan CEM511

career.role: Flour and feed business; later janitor
career.notes: At one time engaged in the flour and feed business in Burlingame; later a janitor in Tekonsha, Calhoun County, Michigan.

narrative_blocks.header: Died suddenly in Tekonsha as a janitor
narrative_blocks.body: Henry P. Aldrich had once worked in the flour and feed business in Burlingame before circumstances took him to Tekonsha, Michigan as a janitor — then he 'died very suddenly the first of the week,' an obituary noted, with his remains sent back to Burlingame to be buried 'beside those of his wife whose death occurred about a year ago.' The 'suicide' notation in genealogical records suggests the death was by his own hand, though the obituary was discreet about the manner — a pattern common in 19th century reporting of such deaths.

sources.label: FindAGrave

narrative_blocks.header: Died at thirty; infant Joseph eight months old
narrative_blocks.body: Ann Hooker Congdon married Joseph Congdon on October 2, 1823 and bore six children in eleven years; her youngest, Joseph, was born April 17, 1834, and she died December 17 that same year at Freedom, New York, at thirty years old — leaving an eight-month-old infant among her surviving children. Her husband Joseph Congdon outlived her by thirty-eight years, dying at Elmira in 1872; the eldest child she had named 'James Hooker' had himself died nine months before that, in May 1865, leaving her family's history as a study in how early death cascades through a generation.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Hooker' honors his mother Ann Hooker Congdon's birth family, descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Hooker; died at Fort Plain at forty
narrative_blocks.body: James Hooker Congdon — his middle name preserving his mother Ann Hooker's birth family — married Jane Augusta Wagner on February 9, 1852, lived at Troy and Fort Plain, New York, and died there on May 25, 1865 at forty years old, his wife surviving him by forty-two years until her own death in 1907 at Johnstown, New York. He was the eldest child of Ann Hooker Congdon, who herself died at thirty at Freedom, New York in 1834 — and his surname Congdon rather than Hooker was the reason his 'Hooker' name migrated to the middle position, a naming pattern repeated throughout the Hooker family tree when daughters married out.

narrative_blocks.header: Which man she married remains uncertain
narrative_blocks.body: EH identified Lucy Hooker's husband as 'Mr. Aldrich,' but FA and the obituary of her sister Elizabeth decisively show Elizabeth married Deach Aldrich — meaning EH appears to have swapped the two husbands, and the correct identity of Lucy's husband is now unknown. She died July 31, 1850 at forty-two, five years after her father Gilbert Hooker died at ninety and outliving him barely — both buried, one presumes, in the upstate New York and Indiana counties where the family had scattered across three generations.

Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

narrative_blocks.header: Her son Fred kept her birth name alive
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Ann Hooker Reed's sixth child, born August 4, 1871, was named Fred Hooker Reed — his middle name preserving his mother's birth surname in a generation that would otherwise have lost it entirely to the Reed name, a naming pattern repeated throughout this family whenever daughters married out of the Hooker line. EH records her marriage date as December 16, 1903, but she died April 16, 1903 — a clear transcription error, as her eldest James Francis was born in October 1856, placing the marriage no later than 1855 or early 1856.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

narrative_blocks.header: She married a man named James Hooker Proctor
narrative_blocks.body: Emma Cordelia Hooker married James Hooker Proctor in 1876 — his middle name 'Hooker' inherited from his mother Lucy (Hooker) Proctor, suggesting his mother also descended from or was named for the Hooker family, though the precise connection remains unresolved. If Lucy Hooker Proctor was herself a descendant of Gilbert Hooker's line, Emma's marriage would be a cousin marriage — two branches of the same family rejoining through the Proctor surname, the 'Hooker' middle name of her husband a clue hiding in plain sight.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

narrative_blocks.header: He possessed Thomas Hooker's clock from England
narrative_blocks.body: When Elizabeth Hooker Aldrich died in Michigan in 1885, her obituary noted that 'a clock owned by Thomas Hooker, brought to this country from England, is in the possession of Mr. Chas. B. Hooker of Grand Rapids, Michigan' — almost certainly Charles Brainerd Hooker, then living in Grand Rapids where his son Roy Zina had been born in 1877.
